# Methodology

## Participants

A total of 97 participants completed the study. 
The experimental group consisted of 48 trilinguals (L1 German, L2 English, L3 Spanish), and the control group included 49 bilinguals (L1 English, L2 Spanish). 
The trilinguals were recruited from Hispanic Linguistics courses in a public university in Frankfurt, Germany. <!-- edited -->
They received extra credit for their participation in the study.
The bilinguals were recruited using Prolific (www.prolific.com) and received monetary compensation for their participation.

(Describe participants and descriptive stats from the questionnaire: nationality, place of residence, sex, age, AoA, etc.)

Participant’s proficiency in English and Spanish was measured via vocabulary size using two lexical tests.
The final groups included participants with a wide range of proficiency scores.

## Materials and Procedure

### Lexical Decision Task
<!-- We need to check and correct the details based on how the task was programmed -->
Participants completed a visual lexical decision task (LDT) in Spanish.
They saw individual strings of letters on a computer screen and indicated whether these strings constituted real words in the Spanish language.
At the beginning of each trial, participants saw the response options "REAL" and "FALSA" on the sides of the screen, then 500ms later, a letter string was displayed in the center of the screen.
Participants responded by pressing the key "1" (REAL) or "0" (FALSA) on a computer keyboard.
Instructions were presented in Spanish, and participants completed a familiarization section with 5 practice trials.
The LDT measured accuracy and response times.
The task was programmed in PsychoPy [@peirce_2019] and delivered online via the Pavlovia platform.

The stimuli of the LDT included a total of 192 items, of which 96 were Spanish pseudowords, 48 were Spanish noncognates, 24 were double cognates (English-Spanish), and 24 were triple cognates (German-English-Spanish). 
The stimulus list can be found in Appendix A.
All items had between 4 and 6 letters.
Pseudowords were created using UniPseudo [@new_etal_2024] and were phonologically and orthographically legal constructions in Spanish. 
Real words were matched for lexical frequency, which was calculated using SUBTLEX-ESP [@cuetos_etal_2011].
Double and triple cognates were orthographically identical.
Orthographic and phonological overlap were calculated using PHOR-In-One [@costa_etal_2023].
Table 1 summarizes the lexical properties of the stimuli items.

```{r, 'stim-properties', echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}

# THIS TABLE NEEDS MANUAL FORMAT ADJUSTMENT 
source(here::here('scripts', '00_libraries.R'))

round2 <- function(x) sprintf("%.2f", x)

stim <- read_csv(here("docs", "manuscript", "tables", "stim_detailed.csv"))

stim_table <- stim %>% 
  mutate(
    type = recode(type,
      "non_cognates" = "Non-cognates",
      "three_way_cognate" = "Triple cognates", 
      "two_way_cognate" = "Double cognates"),
    type = factor(type, levels = c(
      "Non-cognates",
      "Double cognates",
      "Triple cognates"
    ))) %>% 
  group_by(type) %>% 
  summarise(
    across(
      c(length, Subtlex_ES_log10.abs.1.,
        NLD_orthg_AmE_DE, NLD_orthg_AmE_ES, NLD_orthg_DE_ES,
        NLD_phonoL_AmE_DE, NLD_phonoL_AmE_ES, NLD_phonoL_DE_ES),
      ~ paste0(round2(mean(., na.rm = TRUE)), " (", round2(sd(., na.rm = TRUE)), ")")
    )
  ) %>% 
  rename(
    Length = length,
    Frequency = Subtlex_ES_log10.abs.1.,
    ONLD_Eng_Ger = NLD_orthg_AmE_DE,
    ONLD_Eng_Spa = NLD_orthg_AmE_ES,
    ONLD_Ger_Spa = NLD_orthg_DE_ES,
    PNLD_Eng_Ger = NLD_phonoL_AmE_DE,
    PNLD_Eng_Spa = NLD_phonoL_AmE_ES,
    PNLD_Ger_Spa = NLD_phonoL_DE_ES
  )

ft <- flextable(stim_table)

ft <- add_header_row(
  ft,
  values = c("", "", "", "Orthographic NLD", "Phonological NLD"),
  colwidths = c(1, 1, 1, 3, 3)
)

ft <- set_header_labels(
  ft,
  type = "Type",
  Length = "Length",
  Frequency = "Frequency",
  ONLD_Eng_Ger = "English-German",
  ONLD_Eng_Spa = "English-Spanish",
  ONLD_Ger_Spa = "German-Spanish",
  PNLD_Eng_Ger = "English-German",
  PNLD_Eng_Spa = "English-Spanish",
  PNLD_Ger_Spa = "German-Spanish"
)

ft <- set_caption(ft, caption = "Descriptive statistics by stimulus type")
ft <- merge_h(ft, part = "header")
ft <- font(ft, fontname = "Times New Roman", part = "all")
ft <- theme_booktabs(ft)
ft <- align(ft, align = "center", part = "all")
ft <- align(ft, j = 1, align = "left", part = "body")
ft <- autofit(ft)

ft
```

### Proficiency Tests

Participants completed two tests which provided a proxy of language proficiency based on vocabulary size.
English proficiency was measured using the Lexical Test for Advanced Learners of English [LexTALE, @lemhofer_broersma_2012].
The LexTALE is a widely used test that measures vocabulary knowledge via a lexical decision task.
The test includes 60 items, of which 40 are real words and 20 are pseudowords.
The real words include 15 nouns, 12 adjectives, 1 verb, 2 verb participles, 2 adverbs, and 8 items whose form is shared between verbs and nouns (e.g.,
*dispatch*). 
The pseudowords follow the orthographic and phonological rules of English and were designed by changing letters and/or morphemes in real words.
All the test items are between 4 and 12 letters long.
Scoring is based only on accuracy, and scores range from 0 to 100. 
Typically, the LexTALE is completed in no longer than four minutes.

Spanish proficiency was assessed using the LexTALE-ESP [@izura_etal_2014], which follows closely the design of the LexTALE but differs in some aspects. 
The LexTALE-ESP has 90 items, of which 60 are real words and 30 are pseudowords.
The lexical frequency of the real words ranges from very high to very low.
The pseudowords are orthographically and phonologically legal constructions with similar endings to Spanish words from different syntactic categories. 
All items are about 6.6 letters long on average.
Scoring is based on accuracy, and incorrect responses are penalized. 
Scores range from -20 to 60.
This test usually takes less than five minutes to complete.

## Data Analysis

In order to determine whether word type and proficiency impacted both accuracy and reaction time, two main Bayesian models were fit to the data using the brms [@burkner2017brms] package in R. Both models were estimated with Markov chain Monte Carlo sampling using 4 chains, each with 4000 iterations, including 2000 warm-up. Model convergence was evaluated using standard MCMC diagnostics, including trace plots, effective sample sizes, and R-hat values. 


Informative but weakly regularizing priors were specified for the accuracy model to encode theoretically motivated expectations regarding overall accuracy effects by word type. Two-way cognates served as the baseline condition (the intercept) and were centered on an expected baseline accuracy of approximately 80%, whereas condition effects were centered on expected deviations of approximately ±10 percentage points, such that three-way cognates were expected to be more accurate (~90% correct) and non-cognates less accurate (~70% correct) than baseline. Priors remained sufficiently broad to permit substantial updating from the observed data. Random-effects variance parameters were regularized using exponential priors,and participant-level random-effect correlations were assigned an LKJ prior (η=2).

Like the accuracy model, informative priors for log reaction time also used but in this case were based on previously reported data [@szubko-sitarek_2011].
Again, two-way cognates served as the baseline (intercept) for the model and were predicted to be 765ms (sd = .15 in log units). The prior for three-way cognates was a faster reaction time for three-way cognates 743ms) and a slower reaction time for noncognates (819ms), with both standard deviations also set to .15 log units. 


Prior sensitivity analyses were conducted to assess the extent to which our specified priors impacted the reported parameter estimates.
That is, we also report models without prior assumptions akin to a frequentist analysis to determine how much influence the use of priors has on the parameter estimates and thus the narrative conclusions of the paper. 
For each model, an otherwise identical model using default brms priors was fit and compared to the model using theoretically informed priors. Posterior summaries and distributions of focal parameters were compared directly, including posterior means, medians, and 95% credible intervals, alongside condition-level predictions on interpretable scales. Similarity of posterior estimates across models wpuld be interpreted as evidence that inference was driven primarily by the observed data rather than prior assumptions.
